past_medical_history: Recurrent febrile episodes attributed to malaria, treated with artemether/lumefantrine multiple times over the past 3 years
Iron deficiency anemia, diagnosed 1 year ago, currently on oral iron supplementation
History of rheumatic fever at age 12, treated with intramuscular benzathine penicillin prophylaxis until age 18
Palpitations noted intermittently over the past 6 months, not yet evaluated
family_history: Father died suddenly at age 42 while farming; cause of death unknown, assumed to be heatstroke by family
Older brother experienced an episode of fainting during a football match at age 25, not further evaluated

social_history.exercise: Previously active, plays volleyball on university team; has noticed progressive exercise intolerance over the past 4 months with episodes of near-syncope during practice

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate and rhythm. No dysarthria. Attention intact, serial sevens performed accurately (100, 93, 86, 79, 72). Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No post-ictal confusion noted at the time of examination, approximately 30 minutes after the syncopal episode.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex present. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, hip flexors, knee extensors, ankle dorsiflexors. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, and temperature sensation intact in all extremities. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact at bilateral malleoli.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing normal. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or evidence of tongue biting. No urinary incontinence during the episode. Small abrasion on right knee from the fall. Mild bilateral knee joint tenderness on palpation, no effusion.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden loss of consciousness while walking across campus, with brief limb jerking witnessed by a classmate
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Amina Osei is a 21-year-old female university nursing student who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed episode of sudden loss of consciousness approximately 45 minutes ago. She was walking across the university campus with a classmate when she abruptly collapsed without any warning. Her classmate reports that after falling, Ms. Osei exhibited bilateral jerking of her arms and legs lasting approximately 8 to 10 seconds, followed by approximately 20 seconds of motionlessness and pallor. She then opened her eyes and was able to speak coherently within 30 seconds of the onset, asking what happened. She denied any aura, lightheadedness, chest pain, or palpitations preceding the event. There was no tongue biting or urinary incontinence. She was mildly confused for less than one minute before returning to her baseline mental status. This is her third such episode in the past two months. The first episode occurred six weeks ago while she was standing in line at the university cafeteria. She suddenly dropped to the floor without warning, and witnesses described brief shaking movements lasting only a few seconds. She recovered rapidly and attributed the episode to skipping breakfast that morning. The second episode occurred three weeks ago during a volleyball practice session. She collapsed during a sprint drill and was noted to be pale and briefly unresponsive for approximately 15 seconds before waking up confused for less than a minute. After this second event, she visited a local clinic where the physician suspected seizures and prescribed her a short course of diazepam, which she did not take regularly. She has also been experiencing progressive fatigue over the past four months, dyspnea on exertion, and intermittent palpitations, which she attributed to her anemia. She reports intermittent diffuse headaches, mild joint pain in her knees and elbows that she has had for several months, and occasional low-grade fevers. She has been living in a region endemic for malaria and other vector-borne illnesses and reports heavy mosquito exposure at her dormitory. She denies recent travel outside her university town, contact with sick persons, or exposure to animals. She has not had a formal cardiac evaluation and has no known cardiac history aside from her childhood rheumatic fever.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, well-formed and symmetric bilaterally, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, symmetric, no focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with vertex waves, no abnormalities noted during transitions

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric bilateral slowing during hyperventilation, returning to baseline within 20 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response at 10-16 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No electrographic seizures captured during this 40-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording. No events captured during the recording. Interictal sensitivity limited with a single routine EEG.

ecg.rhythm: Sinus bradycardia with Mobitz type II second-degree AV block
findings: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with intermittent 2:1 conduction
Right bundle branch block pattern (rsR' in V1-V2, wide S in I and V6)
Left axis deviation consistent with left anterior fascicular block
Low voltage QRS in limb leads
Non-specific ST segment depression in leads II, III, aVF
ecg.interpretation: Sinus bradycardia with Mobitz type II second-degree AV block and bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block). The combination of Mobitz II AV block with bifascicular block indicates advanced conduction system disease with high risk of progression to complete heart block. QTc prolongation is present and may be partially related to the wide QRS. The non-specific ST changes in inferior leads may reflect underlying structural heart disease. In the context of recurrent syncope without prodrome, this ECG pattern strongly suggests intermittent high-grade AV block as the mechanism.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: White blood cells 11.8 x10^9/L (H), Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 30.2 % (L), MCV 72.1 fL (L), BNP 285 pg/mL (H), CRP 18.4 mg/L (H), Serum Iron 28 mcg/dL (L), Ferritin 8 ng/mL (L), TIBC 420 mcg/dL (H), ESR 48 mm/hr (H), ASO titer 340 IU/mL (H).

valves.Mitral valve: Moderate mitral regurgitation with thickened, restricted posterior leaflet and commissural fusion consistent with rheumatic involvement
valves.Aortic valve: Mild aortic regurgitation with thickened leaflets

output.impression: Abnormal values: Left ventricular ejection fraction 45%  (H), Mitral valve Moderate mitral regurgitation with thickened, restricted posterior leaflet and commissural fusion consistent with rheumatic involvement  (H), Aortic valve Mild aortic regurgitation with thickened leaflets  (H), Left atrium Moderately dilated (4.8 cm) cm (H), Pericardium Trace pericardial effusion  (H).

output.rhythm_summary: Sinus rhythm with intermittent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block progressing to transient complete heart block

events.description: Average heart rate 52 bpm, minimum 28 bpm during a 12-second pause at 03:42 AM
Intermittent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with 2:1 and 3:1 conduction ratios
Three episodes of transient complete (third-degree) AV block lasting 4-12 seconds each with ventricular escape rhythm at 28-32 bpm
Longest pause: 4.8 seconds during an episode of complete heart block at 03:42 AM, associated with patient diary entry of dizziness upon waking
Persistent bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) throughout recording
QTc prolongation (mean 488 ms, maximum 510 ms during bradycardic episodes)
No sustained ventricular tachycardia, rare PVCs (48 total in 24 hours)
No atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter detected

findings: Average heart rate 52 bpm, minimum 28 bpm during a 12-second pause at 03:42 AM
Intermittent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with 2:1 and 3:1 conduction ratios

Longest pause: 4.8 seconds during an episode of complete heart block at 03:42 AM, associated with patient diary entry of dizziness upon waking

QTc prolongation (mean 488 ms, maximum 510 ms during bradycardic episodes)

output.impression: 24-hour Holter monitoring reveals advanced conduction system disease with intermittent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block progressing to transient complete heart block. Three episodes of complete AV block were captured, the longest producing a 4.8-second pause with ventricular escape at 28 bpm. Permanent pacemaker implantation is indicated.

findings.value: Negative for vasovagal syncope. No syncope or presyncope provoked during 30-minute tilt. Noted chronotropic incompetence with blunted heart rate response.

quantitative_data.Test outcome: Negative for vasovagal syncope. No syncope or presyncope provoked during 30-minute tilt. Noted chronotropic incompetence with blunted heart rate response.

output.impression: Abnormal values: Supine heart rate 50 bpm  (H), 70-degree tilt heart rate (5 min) 54 bpm  (H).

findings.description: Baseline: Sinus bradycardia at 50 bpm with bifascicular block
Peak exercise: Heart rate reached only 82 bpm (46% of age-predicted maximum), severe chronotropic incompetence
Exercise terminated at Stage 2 of Bruce protocol due to dyspnea and near-syncope
During recovery: Development of 2:1 AV block with heart rate dropping to 36 bpm
Brief 3.2-second pause during early recovery with presymptomatic lightheadedness

quantitative_data.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with worsening AV conduction during exercise

output.impression: Exercise stress test demonstrates severe chronotropic incompetence with failure to achieve adequate heart rate response. More importantly, worsening AV block was provoked during the recovery phase with 2:1 conduction and a 3.2-second pause. No evidence of myocardial ischemia. The test provides additional evidence supporting the need for permanent pacemaker implantation.

findings.region: Basal inferolateral and inferoseptal segments of the left ventricle
findings.signal: late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall and epicardial enhancement in basal inferolateral wall, consistent with inflammatory or post-inflammatory fibrosis; T2_mapping: Mildly elevated T2 values in the basal septum (54 ms, reference <50 ms), suggesting mild active edema
morphology: Thickened posterior leaflet with restricted motion, commissural fusion

output.impression: Patchy non-ischemic pattern late gadolinium enhancement in the basal inferolateral and inferoseptal walls suggests post-inflammatory myocardial fibrosis, which likely contributes to the conduction system disease. Mildly elevated T2 values in the basal septum raise concern for low-grade active myocardial inflammation. Rheumatic mitral valve morphology is confirmed. LVEF is mildly reduced at 45%. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: cardiac syncope young patient rheumatic heart disease conduction abnormalities

results.title: Conduction System Disease in Rheumatic Heart Disease: A Systematic Review
results.summary: Rheumatic heart disease can affect the conduction system through inflammatory infiltration and fibrosis of the His bundle and bundle branches. AV block occurs in 3-10% of patients with active rheumatic carditis. Complete heart block may develop insidiously and present with syncope.

results.title: Cardiac Syncope vs Epilepsy: Diagnostic Challenges in Resource-Limited Settings
results.summary: Convulsive syncope is frequently misdiagnosed as epilepsy, particularly in resource-limited settings where EEG and cardiac monitoring may not be readily available. Key features distinguishing cardiac syncope include brief duration of convulsive movements (<15 seconds), rapid return to baseline without post-ictal confusion, and absence of tonic-clonic progression.

results.title: Rheumatic Heart Disease in Sub-Saharan Africa: Contemporary Burden and Management
results.summary: Rheumatic heart disease remains highly prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa with an estimated 5.7 million cases. Cardiac conduction abnormalities are an underrecognized complication. In young patients presenting with syncope in endemic regions, the differential should include rheumatic carditis with conduction system involvement.

output.summary: Literature supports that rheumatic heart disease can cause conduction system disease through inflammatory infiltration and fibrosis of the His-Purkinje system. In young patients from endemic regions, cardiac syncope due to rheumatic conduction disease should be considered in the differential for recurrent transient loss of consciousness. Convulsive syncope is commonly misdiagnosed as epilepsy, and ECG with cardiac monitoring is essential for distinguishing these entities.

output.proposed: Warfarin for atrial fibrillation prophylaxis in rheumatic mitral valve disease
interactions: Ferrous sulfate may reduce warfarin absorption; administer at least 2 hours apart
Acetaminophen in doses >2g/day may potentiate warfarin effect and increase INR
warnings: In a young female patient, pregnancy planning must be discussed before initiating warfarin due to teratogenicity
Chloroquine may enhance anticoagulant effect of warfarin; monitor INR closely if both are used
alternatives: Note: Direct oral anticoagulants are NOT recommended for mechanical valve prostheses or rheumatic mitral stenosis

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope secondary to intermittent complete heart block due to rheumatic heart disease with conduction system involvement

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal EEG, very brief convulsive movements (<15 seconds), rapid return to baseline without post-ictal confusion, no tonic-clonic progression, no tongue biting or incontinence
No clear triggers (episodes occur during various activities including standing, exercise, walking), no prodromal symptoms, tilt table test negative

Negative malaria smear and rapid diagnostic test, episodes are recurrent and stereotyped rather than associated with acute febrile illness, low-grade fever may be related to rheumatic activity rather than malaria
While moderate anemia is present, anemia alone rarely causes sudden syncope without prodrome; the ECG findings provide a more specific and dangerous mechanism
Episodes occur in various positions (standing, walking, exercising), no significant orthostatic drop on tilt table testing

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to assess cardiac rhythm and conduction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with bifascicular block, QTc prolongation

optimal_actions.action: Perform routine EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG without epileptiform discharges, arguing against epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, cardiac markers, inflammatory markers, and malaria screening
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate iron deficiency anemia, elevated BNP, elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, ASO titer), negative malaria screen

optimal_actions.action: Request transthoracic echocardiogram for structural heart evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Rheumatic mitral and aortic valve disease, reduced LVEF, left atrial dilation

optimal_actions.action: Request 24-hour Holter monitoring to capture intermittent arrhythmia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Intermittent complete heart block with prolonged pauses, confirming cardiac syncope mechanism

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on cardiac syncope in young patients with rheumatic heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting conduction system involvement in rheumatic heart disease as cause of syncope

optimal_actions.action: Request cardiac MRI for myocardial tissue characterization
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Post-inflammatory myocardial fibrosis, possible active inflammation, confirming rheumatic myocardial involvement

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for anticoagulation in setting of rheumatic valve disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Interactions between warfarin and current medications, teratogenicity warnings

critical_actions: Obtain ECG to identify conduction system disease (Mobitz II AV block with bifascicular block)
Place patient on continuous telemetry monitoring given high-risk ECG findings
Recognize that brief convulsive movements represent convulsive syncope, not epileptic seizures
Obtain echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease given history of rheumatic fever
Urgent cardiology consultation for pacemaker evaluation
Reinitiate benzathine penicillin prophylaxis for secondary prevention of rheumatic fever
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic medications without cardiac evaluation - would delay correct diagnosis and treatment
Discharging patient without continuous cardiac monitoring given high-risk ECG pattern
Administering AV nodal blocking agents (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin) which could worsen heart block
Attributing syncope solely to anemia and treating with iron supplementation alone
Starting chloroquine treatment empirically for malaria without positive testing, which could worsen QTc prolongation
key_reasoning_points: Brief convulsive movements lasting <15 seconds with rapid recovery and no post-ictal state are more consistent with convulsive syncope than epileptic seizures
History of rheumatic fever in childhood should prompt evaluation for rheumatic heart disease including conduction system involvement
ECG showing Mobitz type II AV block with bifascicular block indicates infranodal disease with high risk of progression to complete heart block
Low-grade fever and elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, ASO titer) raise concern for recurrent rheumatic carditis as the etiology of conduction disease
Normal EEG in a patient with recurrent LOC episodes should redirect diagnostic evaluation toward cardiac causes
Elevated BNP in a young patient without known heart failure should prompt echocardiographic evaluation for occult structural heart disease

In endemic regions, malaria and arboviral infections must be considered in the differential for fever with LOC, but negative screening and stereotyped recurrent episodes argue against infectious etiology
Family history of sudden death in the father and sibling syncope may suggest familial pattern of rheumatic susceptibility or unrecognized cardiac disease

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to confounding factors: young patient with history of recurrent febrile illness in malaria-endemic region, iron deficiency anemia as potential alternative explanation for syncope, elevated inflammatory markers suggesting active infection, and convulsive syncope mimicking seizures. The ECG shows clear conduction abnormalities but the clinical context of fever and endemic infectious diseases creates diagnostic uncertainty.
